coredata.dc:title: Photostable electron-transport-layer-free flexible graphene quantum dots/perovskite solar cells by employing bathocuproine interlayer
coredata.dc:creator: Jang, Chan Wook
Shin, Dong Hee
Choi, Suk-Ho
coredata.dc:description: Electron transport layer (ETL)-free perovskite solar cells (PSCs) are very useful for the low-temperature process that can reduce the instability caused by the ETL such as TiO2 layer. There are several publications on ETL-free PSCs, but most of them are based on transparent conductive oxides, not suitable for flexible applications. Here, we first report photostable ETL-free PSCs of maximum 15.72% power conversion efficiency (PCE) by employing an insulating interlayer of bathocuproine (BCP) between the graphene (GR) transparent conductive electrode (TCE) and the graphene quantum dots-added perovskite layer. The BCP interlayer effectively enhances the electron carrier injection by reducing the recombination centers at the active layer/GR TCE interface. Only 8 or 17% of the original PCE is lost under ambient conditions or under light soaking, respectively for 500 h. A flexible-type of the PSC shows a PCE comparable to that of the rigid-type counterpart, and 83% of the initial PCE is maintained even after 1000 repeated bending tests at a radius of 4 mm. These results suggest that the ETL-free PSCs exhibit excellent long-term and mechanical stabilities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite
Solar cell
Electron transport layer
Stability
Bathocuproine
Interlayer
Graphene
Graphene quantum dot
Flexible

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of bathocuproine based perovskite solar cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning ( NRF-2020R1A2C2004376 and NRF-2019R1I1A1A01060800 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic device structure, (b) cross-sectional FE-SEM image, and (c) energy band diagram of a typical ETL-free PSC.
(a) Photo J-V curves, (b) EQE spectra/corresponding integrated Jsc curves, (c) Dark J-V curves, and (d) Jsc-vs-light intensity linearity curves of ETL-free PSCs for various nG.

(a) TRPL decay curves for 4 differently-stacked layers. (b) Nyquist plots for TETA-GR/MAPbI3:GQDs/PTAA/Au PSCs with/without BCP. The electrochemical impedance spectrum was measured in the dark and the equivalent electrical circuit was shown in the inset of (b).
Long-term stabilities of J-V curves and normalized PCE of the ETL-free PSCs (a), (b) in the air and (c), (d) under continuous 1 sun illumination (AM 1.5, 100 m W cm−2, without UV-filter) for 500 h.

(a) J-V characteristics in the forward and reverse scans, (b) EQE spectrum/calculated Jsc curve, (c) Change of normalized PCE as a function of bending cycle at R = 4 mm, and (d) Evolution of relative Rs of the TETA-GR TCE/PET throughout 1000 bending cycles for typical flexible ETL-free PSCs on PETs. The inset in (b) shows a photograph of a typical flexible PSC. The inset in (c) describes the bending tests. The inset in (d) shows plan-view SEM images of the TETA-GR before/after the bending test during 1000 cycles.
Photovoltaic parameters of BCP-based ETL-free PSCs for various nG.
TRPL parameters and calculated CTE values.

head.ce:title: Photostable electron-transport-layer-free flexible graphene quantum dots/perovskite solar cells by employing bathocuproine interlayer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, Institute of Natural Sciences, and Integrated Education Institute for Frontier Science and Technology (BK21 Four), Kyung Hee University, Yongin 17104, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Physics, Institute of Natural Sciences, and Integrated Education Institute for Frontier Science and Technology (BK21 Four), Kyung Hee University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Physics, Institute of Natural Sciences, and Integrated Education Institute for Frontier Science and Technology (BK21 Four), Kyung Hee University, Yongin 17104, Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Andong National University, Andong, Gyeongbuk 36729, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Andong National University, Andong

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Andong National University, Andong, Gyeongbuk, 36729, Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Two authors have contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electron transport layer (ETL)-free perovskite solar cells (PSCs) are very useful for the low-temperature process that can reduce the instability caused by the ETL such as TiO2 layer. There are several publications on ETL-free PSCs, but most of them are based on transparent conductive oxides, not suitable for flexible applications. Here, we first report photostable ETL-free PSCs of maximum 15.72% power conversion efficiency (PCE) by employing an insulating interlayer of bathocuproine (BCP) between the graphene (GR) transparent conductive electrode (TCE) and the graphene quantum dots-added perovskite layer. The BCP interlayer effectively enhances the electron carrier injection by reducing the recombination centers at the active layer/GR TCE interface. Only 8 or 17% of the original PCE is lost under ambient conditions or under light soaking, respectively for 500 h. A flexible-type of the PSC shows a PCE comparable to that of the rigid-type counterpart, and 83% of the initial PCE is maintained even after 1000 repeated bending tests at a radius of 4 mm. These results suggest that the ETL-free PSCs exhibit excellent long-term and mechanical stabilities.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decade, intensive studies have been done on hybrid perovskite solar cells (PSCs), thereby leading to remarkable enhancement of the power conversion efficiency (PCE) over the commercial level [1–3], but several obstacles should be overcome before the successful applications. In particular, PSCs are vulnerable to heat, humidity, and light, resulting in long-term instabilities [4–6]. Proper encapsulation technology can be employed to protect PSCs from heat and humidity, but it is not easy to keep their photovoltaic performance stable even under long-term light soaking. The degradation of PSCs under illumination is driven in the perovskite film as well as in the adjacent films in contact with the active layer. PSCs are commonly classified into n-i-p- (normal) and p-i-n- (inverted) type architectures [7,8]. During solar irradiation, electron/hole carriers generated in the perovskite layer are transported through electron-/hole-transport layer (ETL/HTL) and are eventually collected in the cathode/anode, respectively. Metal oxides such as TiO2 and ZnO are widely used as ETLs in n-i-p type PSCs for high PCE [9–12]. Typically, TiO2 layer requires high-temperature heat treatment for the intimate contact and has a drawback of decomposing the organic groups in the active layer due to the photo-induced instability [4,5,13]. To reduce these problems, a lot of efforts were made by employing a passivation technique or locating another n-type oxide at the TiO2/perovskite layer interface, but they were not fully solved [14–16].

Based on these considerations, one of the best ways to keep the photovoltaic performance of PSCs more stable is to remove the ETL layer [17–25]. ETL-free PSCs are very useful not only for the cost reduction but also for the low-temperature process that can reduce the instability [25]. There have been several reports on the fabrication and characterization of ETL-free PSCs, whose PCEs are much smaller than those of conventional PSCs [17,18,21,22,24]. No attempts have been made to employ graphene (GR) transparent conductive electrodes (TCEs) in ETL-free PSCs, especially useful for their flexible counterparts. In previous literature, we successfully prepared triethylenetetramine (TETA)-doped GR (TETA-GR) TCE showing good flexible and elastic stabilities in air in addition to the excellent TCE performance [26,27]. The addition of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to the perovskite active layer was also shown to improve the performance of the photoelectric devices [28]. Here, we first report concomitant use of TETA-GR, GQDs, and bathocuproine (BCP) for photostable ETL-free PSCs of maximum 15.72% PCE, where the BCP plays as an insulating interlayer between the TETA-GR TCE and the GQDs-added perovskite active layer. The PSCs lost only 8 or 17% of the original PCE under ambient conditions or under light soaking, respectively for 500 h. We also fabricated a flexible type of the PSC whose PCE is comparable to that of the rigid-type counterpart, with 83% of the original PCE being maintained even after 1000 repeated bending tests at a radius of curvature (R) of 4 mm, suggesting excellent mechanical stability.

GR was synthesized on Cu foil in a thermal furnace at 1000 °C for 5 min under Ar:H2:CH4 mixed-gas flow. Subsequently, the pre-prepared poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA, Sigma Aldrich) solution was dropped all over the GR surface, spin-coated at 4500 rpm for 1 min, and then heat treated on a hot plate at 180 °C for 1 min. Here, the PMMA solution was prepared by adding 0.46 g PMMA powder to 10 mL chlorobenzene (Sigma Aldrich) and stirring the mixture at 60 °C for 10 h. The Cu was removed by dipping the PMMA/GR/Cu foil stack in 1 M FeCl3 solution (Sigma Aldrich). The PMMA/GR stack was then transferred onto glass or PET substrates and heat-treated at 120 for 2 h. Finally, the PMMA was dissolved by acetone. For n-type doping of GR, the TFSA solution was spin-coated on the GR sheet at 2500 rpm for 1 min and then heat treated at 100 °C for 1 min. Here, the TETA molecules (Sigma-Aldrich) was dissolved in ethanol to prepare the TETA solution of 0.1 mol/L concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of bathocuproine based perovskite solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate ETL-free PSCs, the BCP dissolved in isopropyl alcohol (0.5 mg/mL) was spin-coated at 1500 rpm for 40 s on pre-prepared GR or TETA-GR substrates. GQDs powder was dispersed in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) to make the GQDs solution at various concentrations (nG) = 5, 10, and 20 mg/L. The perovskite precursor solution was prepared by dissolving the CH3NH3I and PbI2 powders (1:1 ratio) in a mixed solvent of dimethyl sulfoxide and DMF (2:8 vol ratio). Then, the GQDs/perovskite-mixed (MAPbI3:GQDs) solution was dropped on the BCP layer, and spin-coated at 4000 rpm for 20 s. At 10 s after the start of the spin-coating, 0.5-mL antisolvent was slowly dropped onto the rotating substrate. The coated substrate was heat-treated at 100 °C for 5 min to finally obtain the MAPbI3:GQDs layer, which was then cooled to room temperature. PTAA hole transport material (HTM) was spin-coated on the MAPbI3:GQDs layer at 3000 rpm for 30 s. For the HTM solution, Li-bis- (trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide/acetonitrile (7.5 μL, 170 mg/mL) and 4-tert-butylpyridine /acetonitrile (7.5 μL, 1:1) additives were mixed in the PTAA/toluene (15 mg/mL). Finally, Au metal was thermally evaporated on the PTAA layer to form the back contact. The active area of all the cells was defined by using a shadow mask of 16 mm2.

The surface roughness of the GR and BCP layers were measured by atomic force microscope in non-contact mode (AFM, Park Systems, model XE-100). The transmittance (T) of the GR transferred onto a glass substrate was measured from 300 to 900 nm by an optical spectrometer (Agilent Varian, model Carry 5000). The sheet resistance (Rs), mobility, and work function of the GR were obtained by the 4-probe van der Pauw method (Keithley, model 4200-SCS), Kelvin probe force microscope (Park Systems, model XE-100), and Hall effect measurement (Ecopia, Model HEM-2000), respectively. Here, the measured work function of the GR was corrected by using Au metal as a reference. The size and distribution of the GQDs were analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) with electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) mapping. Plan- and cross-sectional-view images of the materials and devices were obtained by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Carl Zeiss, model LEO SUPRA 55). The absorption and X-ray-diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained for the samples on glass substrates by optical spectrometer (Agilent Varian, model Carry 5000) and by GADDS system (Bruker), respectively. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of the perovskite films were measured in a system with 470 nm pulsed laser line. Current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics were monitored for forward/reverse scans at 200 ms and 10 mV under standard conditions. Photovoltaic characterizations were done in a solar simulator (McScience, model K201) under 100 mW/cm2 (Air mass 1.5 G) illumination. External quantum efficiency (EQE) of the cells were measured by using a white light source (Newport, 300 W xenon lamp) in a monochromator (Dongwoo Optron, model MonoRa500i)-connected instrument. The bending test of the flexible cells was conducted at a 0.5 Hz frequency.

Fig. 1(a–b) shows a schematic diagram and a cross-sectional FE-SEM image of a typical planar-type MAPbI3 PSC structure of TETA-GR/BCP/MAPbI3:GQDs/PTAA/Au. Fig. 1(c) shows an energy band diagram of the PSC, drawn by using the band energies of the constituent layers, as shown in our previous reports [27,29]. Here, the low energy barrier (~0.3 eV) formed at the BCP/ MAPbI3:GQDs interface could hinder the collection of the electrons generated in the active layer to the TETA-GR TCE. Structural, electrical, and optical properties of the GR TCE were evaluated before/after TETA doping. The surface roughness (Rq) of the GR TCE was varied from 0.36 to 0.75 nm after the doping (Supporting Information, Fig. S1a–b), indicating the GR surface is smooth, regardless of the doping. The work function was 4.58 eV for the bare GR, meaning weak p-type, but after TETA doping, it decreased to 4.43 eV, in other words, the TETA-GR is n-type (Supporting Information, Table S1). The TETA dopants induce n-type doping by donating electrons in the GR [26,27]. The mobility of the GR was reduced from 2050 to 1260 cm2 V−1 s−1 by the doping. The T of the GR at a 550 nm wavelength showed a slight decrease from 97.2% to 96.3% even after the doping (Supporting Information, Fig. S1c). On the other hand, the Rs of the bare GR was 670 Ω/sq, but after doping, it was greatly reduced to 220 Ω/sq (Supporting Information, Table S1), consistent with the previously-reported results [26,27]. The Rq of the BCP film coated on the GR surface was 2.75 nm, and its thickness was measured to be ~30 nm (Supporting Information, Fig. S2). As analyzed by low-magnification TEM images, the diameter of the GQDs was distributed in the range of 3.8–7.8 nm (Supporting Information, Fig. S3a). The K-edge EELS spectrum of the GQDs was peaked at ~285 and ~291 eV (Supporting Information, Fig. S3b), corresponding to the π*and σ* states, respectively, originating from the GQDs [28].

To increase the size of the crystallites in the perovskite thin film, the GQDs were mixed with the perovskite film for various nG. By SEM images (Supporting Information, Fig. S4), the grain size was shown to be in the range of 50–200 nm at nG = 0 mg/L (bare perovskite film), and it increased to ~450 nm at nG = 10 mg/L. Due to the hydrophilic groups such as -OH, -COOH, and C = O at the periphery of the GQDs [28], the GQDs possibly help form strong bonds in the perovskite film, leading to best crystallization at nG = 10 mg/L. On the other hand, at nG = 20 mg/L, imperfections such as pinholes, grain boundaries, and other defects were formed in the perovskite film due to the excess GQDs, resulting in the degradation of the crystallinity (Supporting Information, Fig. S4). In the XRD spectra (Supporting Information, Fig. S5a), the peaks corresponding to the (110) and (220) crystal facets were clearly observed in all the GQDs-added perovskite films, typical of a tetragonal structure. In particular, the (100) peak intensity/FWHM are highest/smallest at nG = 10 mg/L, respectively (Supporting Information, Fig. S5b), indicating the best crystallinity, consistent with the SEM images. In the ultraviolet (UV)-visible absorption spectra measured in the range from 300 to 900 nm (Supporting Information, Fig. S5c), the absorption intensity was shown to be highest at nG = 10 mg/L. The PL spectra of the MAPbI3:GQDs were peaked at ~775 nm, regardless of nG (Supporting Information, Fig. S5d). These results suggest that the band structure of the MAPbI3 is almost not influenced by the inclusion of the GQDs.

Fig. 2(a) shows photo J-V curves of the PSCs for various nG. The bare-perovskite cell (nG = 0 mg/L) shows 0.87/0.94 V open-circuit voltage (Voc), 16.93/17.98 mA/cm2 short-current density (Jsc), and 53.02/68.98% fill factor (FF), 7.81/11.66% PCE for forward/reverse scans, respectively. With moderate amount of GQDs incorporated into the perovskite film, all the photovoltaic parameters are significantly enhanced, as summarized in Table 1 (Supporting Information, Fig. S6). Specifically, the PSCs exhibit maximum parameters of 0.97/0.99 V Voc, 19.44/20.65 mA-cm−2 Jsc, 70.45/76.90% FF, and 13.28/15.72% PCE at nG = 10 mg/L for the forward/reverse scans, respectively, comparable to or even higher than those of previous reports (Supporting Information, Table S2) [18–24]. Statistical deviations of the J-V curves for 10 devices result in the average PCE of 12.57 ± 0.71/14.87 ± 0.85% for forward/reverse scans, respectively at nG = 10 mg/L. By increasing the cell size 500 times from 16 mm2 to 80 cm2, the PCE showed only 31.8/23.1% decreases for forward/reverse scans, respectively. As shown in the FE-SEM images (Supporting Information, Fig. S4d) and XRD spectra (Supporting Information, Fig. S5b), the crystalline properties of the MAPbI3:GQDs layer are the best at nG = 10 mg/L. This explains why the photovoltaic parameters are maximized at nG = 10 mg/L.

Especially, the nG-dependent behavior of the Voc can be explained as follows. How effectively the electron-hole pairs are generated and the carrier recombination is suppressed are the two main factors to determine the Voc. As shown in the UV–visible absorption spectra (Supporting Information, Fig. S5c), the addition of the GQDs maximally improves the light absorption of the active layer at nG = 10 mg/L, thereby contributing to the increase of the photogenerated electrons/holes. The number of carrier-recombination centers in the active layer is minimized at nG = 10 mg/L, judging from the best crystalline quality of the active layer at nG = 10 mg/L, as shown in the FE-SEM and XRD data. On the other hand, the average PCE of the bare-perovskite (nG = 0 mg/L) cell without BCP is 5.43 ± 1.34/7.89 ± 1.67% for the forward/reverse scans, respectively (Supporting Information, Fig. S7a), significantly smaller than those of the counterpart with BCP. The average PCE of the bare-GR cell with BCP is 6.51 ± 0.82/7.32 ± 1.86% for forward/reverse scans, respectively (Supporting Information, Fig. S7b), possibly resulting from the high Rs of the bare GR (670 Ω/sq) (Supporting Information, Table S1). Fig. 2(b) shows EQE spectra from 300 to 900 nm for various nG. The nG-dependent variation of the integrated EQE is consistent with that of the Jsc [30], as shown in Table 1.

To further check why best PCE was achieved at nG = 10 mg/L, additional electrical measurements were done to obtain dark J-V curves, as shown in Fig. 2(c). From the slopes of the dark J-V curves for various nG, shunt resistance (Rsh), ideality factor (n), and series resistance (Rse) were calculated [31]. Resulting Rsh/Rse values were approximately 2140/5.12, 9795/2.60, 12,672/1.83, and 4864/3.42 for 0, 5, 10, and 20 mg/L, respectively. At nG = 10 mg/L, the Rsh is largest whilst the Rse is smallest, leading to the highest FF. The n is minimized to 2.24 at nG = 10 mg/L, as summarized in Table 1. These results suggest that the cells show optimized diode properties at nG = 10 mg/L, resulting in the best photovoltaic parameters. We also measured Jsc for various solar intensities (I) from 10 to 100 mW/cm2 to evaluate the carrier recombination process (Fig. 2(d)). The Jsc vs I curves were fitted based on the relation: Jsc ∝ Iα, where α is the exponent value [32]. The calculated α value is maximized to 0.97 at nG = 10 mg/L, indicating smallest recombination loss at nG = 10 mg/L, consistent with the nG-dependent diode properties, as shown in Fig. 2(c).

The PSCs are composed of various layers where dipole moments can be induced intrinsically or extrinsically, thereby increasing their built-in potential and subsequently leading to the enhancement of the electric field for the separation of photo-generated excitons and the facilitation of charge extraction. [33,34] Especially, the edges of GQDs are known to be terminated with various kinds of functional groups, [35,36] such as amide, cyano, isopropyl, carboxyl, nitro, aldehyde, fluorine, and oxygen, which can induce considerable dipole moments. [37,38] Therefore, it is possibly thought that the dipole moments at the periphery of the GQDs within the perovskites influence the built-in potential of the GQDs/PSCs, maximizing the PCE at nG = 10 mg/L.

To more systematically identify the role of the BCP in the cell, we measured the PL decay of the perovskite film for four different layer combinations of glass/perovskite, glass/BCP/perovskite, glass/TETA-GR/perovskite, and glass/TETA-GR/BCP/perovskite, as shown in Fig. 3(a). Here, nG was fixed at 10 mg/L. The decay time was calculated from the TRPL decay curves through the fitting with single-exponential functions [39–42] to compare the movement of the carriers for the four layered structures on glass substrate. By including the BCP, the PL decay becomes slower (527 → 597 ns) in the glass/perovskite structure whilst it becomes much faster (229 → 130 ns) in the TETA-GR/perovskite structure, as summarized in Table 2. These results suggest that the BCP interlayer effectively controls the electron carrier injection between the perovskite and the TETA-GR, but not between the perovskite and the glass. Charge-transfer efficiency (CTE) can be calculated based on the equation: CTE = 1 - τDA/τD, where τDA and τD are the PL lifetimes of the cells with/without an acceptor (here, the TETA-GR TCE plays as an electron acceptor), respectively [43]. The CTE values for the cells with/without BCP are calculated to be 78.22/56.55%, respectively (Table 2), which explains how important the BCP interlayer is for enhancing the PCE in the ETL-free PSCs. To further investigate the effect of the BCP on the interfacial charge transfer in the PSCs, we performed electrochemical impedance spectrum measurements. Fig. 3(b) shows Nyquist plots of impedance spectral behaviors for the TETA-GR/MAPbI3:GQDs/PTAA/Au cell with/without BCP at nG = 10 mg/L. The BCP-cell exhibits smaller charge transfer resistance (RCT) than the BCP-free cell, indicating smooth charge transfer due to the presence of the BCP layer between the TETA-GR and the MAPbI3. These results further suggest that the low energy barrier at the BCP/active layer, as shown in Fig. 1(c), does not give a considerable effect on the collection of the electrons to the GR TCE, but more dominantly, the BCP interlayer reduces the recombination centers between the perovskite and the TETA-GR [25].

Long-term behaviors of the cells without encapsulation were monitored for 500 h under ambient conditions as well as under continuous light soaking. Fig. 4(a) shows the variation of the J-V behavior for 500 h in air. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the cell lost only 8% of the original PCE value from 15.63% to 14.38% for 500 h. Fig. 4(c) shows temporal change of the J-V curve under continuous AM 1.5 illumination without a UV filter for 500 h at 60 °C/30% humidity conditions. Initially (t = 0 h), the photovoltaic parameters of the cell: Voc, Jsc, FF, and PCE were 0.99 V, 20.64 mA/cm2, 76.70%, and 15.68%, respectively. After 500 h, the PCE was 17% degraded (absolutely 15.68–13.02%) (Fig. 4(d)), indicating remarkable long-term stability compared to previously-reported ETL-free PSCs [21,24].

In the final step, we prepared flexible PSCs with the same structure at nG = 10 mg/L by using PET substrates. Fig. 5(a) shows J-V characteristics of the flexible PSC. The extracted photovoltaic parameters are 0.94/0.99 V Voc, 18.21/19.75 mA /cm2 Jsc, 68.48/74.12% FF, and 11.72/14.49% PCE for forward/reverse scans, respectively. Fig. 5(b) shows the EQE spectrum from 300 to 900 nm of the flexible cell. The integrated Jsc from the EQE spectrum is 18.81 mA/cm2, consistent with the measured Jsc. The mechanical stability of the flexible cell was evaluated by repeated bending test (Supporting Information, Fig. S8). Fig. 5(c) shows the variation of the PCE by repeated bending up to 1000 times for R = 4 mm. More than 82% of the initial PCE value is maintained even after the bending test. To clarify the cause of the device deterioration, we also monitored the evolution of the Rs for the TETA-GR/PET in the same way. After 1000 cycles, the relative Rs of the TETA-GR (ΔRs/Rs) increased by 25%, as shown in Fig. 5(d), which can be interpreted by the cracking of the GR TCE, as shown in the plan-view SEM image. These results suggest that the ETL-free PSCs are very promising as a flexible power source.

Photostable ETL-free PSCs of 15.72% maximum PCE were successfully fabricated based on a structure of TETA-GR/BCP/MAPbI3:GQDs/PTAA/Au. The BCP played as a role of interlayer for effectively reducing the recombination centers at the TETA-GR/the active layer interface, resulting in the enhancement of the electron carrier injection. The cells showed excellent long-term stabilities by losing only 8 or 17% of the original PCE under ambient conditions or under continuous light soaking, respectively for 500 h. Flexible PSCs with the same structure showed similar maximum PCE with the rigid-type counterpart, and maintained 83% of the initial PCE even after 1000 repeated bending tests, indicating excellent mechanical stability.

C. W. Jang and D. H. Shin performed device design, device fabrication, and characterization for the solar cells. S.-H. Choi initiated, supervised the work, and wrote the paper. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (NRF-2020R1A2C2004376 and NRF-2019R1I1A1A01060800).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.161355.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stable perovskite solar cells with efficiency exceeding 24.8% and 0.3-V voltage loss

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Jeong, I. W. Choi, E. M. Go, Y. Cho, M. Kim, B. Lee, S. Jeong, Y. Jo, H. W. Choi, J. Lee, J.-H. Bae, S. K. Kwak, D. S. Kim, C. Yang, Stable perovskite solar cells with efficiency exceeding 24.8% and 0.3-V voltage loss, Science 369 (2020) 1615‐1620.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pseudo-halide anion engineering for α-FAPbI3 perovskite solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Jeong, M. Kim, J. Seo, H. Lu, P. Ahlawat, A. Mishra, Y. Yang, M. A. Hope, F. T. Eickemeyer, M. Kim, Y. J. Yoon, I. W. Choi, B. Primera Darwich, S. J. Choi, Y. Jo, J. H. Lee, B. Walker, S. M. Zakeeruddin, L. Emsley, U. Rothlisberger, A. Hagfeldt, D. S. Kim, M. Grätzel, J. Y. Kim, Pseudo-halide anion engineering for α-FAPbI3 perovskite solar cells, Nature 592 (2021) 381‐385.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient perovskite solar cells via improved carrier management

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. J. Yoo, G. Seo, M. R. Chua, T. G. Park, Y. Lu, F. Rotermund, Y.-K. Kim, C. S. Moon, N. J. Jeon, J.-P. Correa-Baena, V. Bulović, S. S. Shin, M. G. Bawendi, J. Seo, Efficient perovskite solar cells via improved carrier management, Nature 590 (2021) 587‐593.
